disease.name: Physiological Jaundice of the Newborn
disease.note: Most common cause of neonatal jaundice. A normal transitional phenomenon due to physiologic hepatic immaturity. NEVER appears in first 24h of life (if it does, suspect hemolytic disease until proven otherwise).

immediateDangerSigns.sign: jaundice appearing in first 24 hours of life
total bilirubin crossing exchange transfusion threshold per Bhutani nomogram
signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (lethargy, hypotonia, high-pitched cry, opisthotonos)

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Low risk: Observation, no treatment needed. Moderate risk: Phototherapy per Bhutani nomogram thresholds. Severe risk: Exchange transfusion.

historyFeatures.symptomId: good feeding and normal activity in an otherwise healthy newborn
jaundice first noted between 24-72 hours of life

firstLine: Bhutani nomogram plot (risk stratification by gestational age)
Blood type and Rh of infant and mother
Direct antiglobulin test (DAT / Coombs test) to exclude isoimmunization
secondLine: End-tidal carbon monoxide (ETCOc) measurement if available
investigations.goldStandard: Serial total serum bilirubin plotted on Bhutani nomogram with gestational age-specific risk stratification

emergency: Exchange transfusion if bilirubin exceeds exchange transfusion threshold per Bhutani nomogram or if signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy present
Intensive phototherapy (double/triple surface) while preparing exchange transfusion
IVIG 0.5-1 g/kg if isoimmune hemolytic disease with bilirubin rising despite phototherapy
inpatient: Intensive phototherapy for levels above phototherapy threshold per Bhutani nomogram
Ensure adequate hydration (IV fluids if dehydrated or if significant insensible losses from phototherapy)
Monitor bilirubin q4-8h depending on rate of rise and proximity to exchange threshold
Regular monitoring of temperature and hydration status
outpatient: No treatment needed for low-risk levels
Ensure adequate feeding (breastfeeding 8-12 times per day or formula feeding)
Reassurance and education for parents about signs of worsening jaundice
Outpatient follow-up within 24-48h for borderline cases
Sunlight exposure is NOT recommended (risk of sunburn, overheating, dehydration)
refractory: Consider underlying hemolytic process if rapidly rising or failing phototherapy
Exchange transfusion for levels refractory to intensive phototherapy
Evaluate for G6PD deficiency, hereditary spherocytosis, other hemolytic anemias
preventive: Universal predischarge bilirubin screening (transcutaneous or serum)
Risk assessment before discharge using Bhutani nomogram and risk factors
Early and frequent feeding to promote stooling and bilirubin excretion
Timely follow-up, especially for infants discharged <72h of age
Breastfeeding support to prevent dehydration and excessive weight loss

disease.pathophysiology: Physiologic hepatic immaturity leads to reduced UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (UGT1A1) activity causing decreased bilirubin conjugation. This is combined with shortened RBC lifespan (80 days vs 120 days in adults) creating increased bilirubin load from RBC turnover. The net imbalance between bilirubin production and clearance results in transient unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia that typically resolves as hepatic enzyme maturation occurs over the first 1-2 weeks of life.
complications: Severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia leading to acute bilirubin encephalopathy
Kernicterus (chronic bilirubin encephalopathy) with choreoathetoid cerebral palsy, upward gaze palsy, hearing loss, dental enamel dysplasia
mustNotMiss: Jaundice appearing in first 24 hours of life (hemolytic disease until proven otherwise)
Direct/conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (>2 mg/dL or >20% of total) suggests obstructive or hepatocellular pathology
Signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy require emergency exchange transfusion
Prolonged jaundice beyond 2 weeks in term or 3 weeks in preterm requires evaluation for pathologic cause

diagnosticClues.clue: Jaundice first appears between 24-72 hours of life in an otherwise healthy, well-feeding newborn
Unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin predominates with normal direct fraction
Total bilirubin levels within Bhutani nomogram low-risk or medium-risk zone
Healthy active infant with normal feeding, normal neurological exam, and normal tone
Spontaneous resolution without intervention in low-risk cases by day 10-14 of life

exclusionClues.clue: Jaundice appearing within first 24 hours of life (pathologic until proven otherwise)
Direct/conjugated bilirubin >2 mg/dL or >20% of total bilirubin
Total bilirubin >15 mg/dL in term infant without risk factors
Signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (lethargy, hypotonia, high-pitched cry, opisthotonos)
Jaundice persisting beyond 2 weeks in term or 3 weeks in preterm infant
